title: Render-to-temp + copy breaks merge/splice on-conflict against the working tree
body: If a projection renders into a fresh temp dir and the rendered output is then copied into the working tree, any merge-with-existing semantics in the projecting code (merge-managed-key-only for JSON, preserve-outside-block for managed-block-inline) sees only the empty temp dir — the merge target is always blank — so the working tree's unrelated keys / outside-block content gets clobbered on the copy step. For real-write modes, project DIRECTLY into the working tree so the merge/splice code sees the existing state. For dry-run, clone the relevant subset of the working tree into a shadow temp dir first, project into the clone, then diff clone vs working tree. Bit us in the post-EXECUTE review of RFC-0001's distribution-adapters spec; the unit tests passed because they re-ran into the same output dir (which carried the prior render), but the integrated --self path broke silently.

title: shutil.copytree follows symlinks by default — supply-chain hazard for published artifacts
body: `shutil.copytree(src, dst)` defaults to `symlinks=False`, which means symlinks in `src` are DEREFERENCED — the link target's contents land in `dst`. Same for `shutil.copy2` (default `follow_symlinks=True`). When projecting source files that may be attacker-controlled (third-party packs, plugin submissions, untrusted catalogues) into a published output tree, a malicious symlink to `/etc/passwd` exfiltrates the build host's secrets into the artifact. Pass `symlinks=True` to copytree and `follow_symlinks=False` to copy2 — they preserve symlinks as symlinks rather than dereferencing. Caught by the security-reviewer pass on RFC-0001's distribution pipeline; not exploitable today (packs are repo-owned) but lethal the day production packs from PRs are admitted.

title: Document a JSON-Schema subset in spec + plan + validator docstring, all three in sync
body: A stdlib-only Python validator forces a documented subset (no $ref, no oneOf, etc.). The spec's AC needs to enumerate the subset; the plan's task Approach needs to enumerate it; the validator's module docstring needs to enumerate it. If any of the three drifts, a future agent extends one and not the others, and the next adversarial review surfaces it as a Blocker. Worth a single source of truth across docs + code from day one. Applies to any RFC where stdlib-only is a hard constraint and a subset of an external standard is the contract.

title: Rename audits must enumerate docstring + comment + error-message sites, not just code constants
body: When a rename touches a name-that-is-an-identifier (filename, directory, package), the per-task line-level audit in the plan must list every string-literal occurrence — including module docstrings, header comments, assertion messages, help strings, and CLI-arg examples — not just code constants. The brief's `grep` Done-when test surfaces every site; the plan's per-task list is what executes against it. If the plan enumerates only constants, docstrings drift past EXECUTE and the post-EXECUTE adversarial-reviewer raises them as Blockers. Caught on PR#22 (relocate adapter contract to docs/contracts/): the round-1 plan listed only `CONTRACT_PATH` / `SCHEMA_PATH` constants in T3/T4; round-2 post-EXECUTE review found 14 docstring + assertion-message hits the audit had missed. Fix: when the reference taxonomy says 'Class X always rewrites', the per-task audit's grep must cover docstrings, comments, and message strings in the same sweep.

title: TOML datetime round-trip injection: a bare-datetime field can come back as `str` and carry phantom payload on re-emit
body: When a TOML emitter writes `installed-at = 2026-01-01T00:00:00Z` (bare offset-datetime literal), `tomllib.loads` parses that as a Python `datetime`. If a *different* writer (adversarial, hand-edited, third-party) writes `installed-at = "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z\nphantom = \"injected\""` (TOML basic-string position instead of bare datetime), `tomllib` parses the same key as a Python `str` containing a real newline and quote bytes. Bare re-emission `f"installed-at = {entry['installed-at']}"` then lands phantom TOML structure on the next round-trip — even though the first-write side is clean. The fix has two layers: (1) filter prior entries at read time, requiring `isinstance(value, datetime)` for any field that's spec'd as a bare datetime; drop or warn on mismatch. (2) Store new entries as `datetime` (not a strftime'd string) so the emit loop has one uniform type and always applies the canonical strftime itself. Bit us during round-3 adversarial review of the TOML-injection hardening PR; the pack-metadata side was already closed but the marker round-trip path was a separate vector. Generalises to any TOML field where the schema says 'bare type X' but the loader will accept 'basic-string containing arbitrary bytes' at the same key.

title: Bash-to-Python port: heredoc rc was normalised; naked `return violations` leaks the raw count
body: When bash invokes a Python heredoc as `python3 - args <<PY ... PY` and the heredoc ends with `sys.exit(1 if violations else 0)`, the heredoc's rc is captured by `$?` as exactly 0 or 1 — bash callers everywhere pattern-match on `rc -eq 1`. A naive port that hoists the heredoc body into a Python function returning `violations` (an int count) and `sys.exit(<count>)` at the top level silently widens the rc shape: with 2+ violations, rc=2; rc>125 is reserved territory. Callers like `tools/test-*.sh`'s `[[ $got -eq 1 ]]` or a CI `if rc == 1` branch silently miss the failure. Aggregators that only check non-zero (like our `pre-pr.py`) mask this further. The fix is one line: `return 1 if violations else 0` at every exit site. Bit us in the post-EXECUTE review of windows-hooks-phase3; lint-build.py's two return paths both leaked the count. Generalises to any bash-heredoc-to-Python-function port — the parity-pinning bar is the rc *shape*, not just "non-zero on failure".

title: Bash-to-Python port: `"foo" in path.parts` matches segment at *any* depth where bash `-not -path './foo/*'` was top-level-anchored
body: Bash `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*'` is anchored at the leading `./` — only TOP-level `node_modules` or `.git` are excluded. The Python equivalent `if "node_modules" in path.parts` excludes a `node_modules` segment at ANY depth, so a nested `packages/x/node_modules/y/AGENTS.md` is silently skipped where bash would still apply the per-package lint. The divergence is invisible until someone adds a nested vendor dir, at which point lint coverage shrinks without warning. The fix: `if path.parts[:1] in (("node_modules",), (".git",))` — anchor to position 0. Surfaced in the post-EXECUTE adversarial review of windows-hooks-phase3's lint-agents-md.py port. The general rule for path-glob transliteration: read the bash glob as anchored (`./X/*` = top-level) or unanchored (`*/X/*` = any depth) and pick the Python predicate that matches the anchor, not the substring.

title: Cross-surface parity (lint vs drift gate): `bytes.strip()` and `str.strip()` are not equivalent — Unicode whitespace creates a silent acceptance gap
body: When two checking surfaces claim parity over the same shape — e.g. `lint-agents-md.py` accepting both real symlinks and a one-line file containing `"AGENTS.md"` for the repo-root CLAUDE.md alias, and `self_host.py`'s drift detector being asked to match — the natural Python idioms `bytes.strip()` and `str.strip()` differ in what counts as whitespace. `bytes.strip()` only strips ASCII whitespace (`\t \n \r \v \f` + space); `str.strip()` strips Unicode whitespace too (NBSP U+00A0, IDEOGRAPHIC SPACE, etc.). A `CLAUDE.md` containing `"\xc2\xa0AGENTS.md\xc2\xa0"` (NBSP-padded) passes a `read_text(...).strip() == "AGENTS.md"` lint but fails a `disk_bytes.strip() == b"AGENTS.md"` gate. The realistic window is narrow (no Windows checkout produces NBSP padding) but the docstring/spec claim 'mirrors check #2's strip() semantics' becomes a lie unless the gate decodes first: `disk_bytes.decode("utf-8", errors="replace").strip() == "AGENTS.md"`. Surfaced in round-2 adversarial review of AC15b (PR #77). General rule: when two surfaces claim parity, test both the acceptance side AND the rejection-boundary side, and pick predicates from the same domain (both bytes or both str) — not the convenient mix.

title: Don't wire any lint into the projected pre-pr.py; a lint CAN ship to adopters as a skill script
body: Never wire a lint into tools/hooks/pre-pr.py: it's PROJECTED to adopters (sourced from packs/core/.apm/hooks/pre-pr.py), so it would call a script absent from their tree. That principle stands. But the corollary 'linters don't project, so they must be catalogue-only in tools/' is FALSE (RFC-0016 originally claimed it; corrected in RFC-0016 § Errata + ADR-0007). A skill's scripts/ folder DOES project to every adapter and already ships governance Python (work-loop/scripts/loop-cohort.py, new-adr/new-rfc scripts/next-ordinal.py). The doc-drift spec-metadata lint now lives at packs/core/.apm/skills/work-loop/scripts/lint-spec-status.py — agent-invoked at the work-loop finish-time checklist (available, not fail-closed), AND run by the catalogue as a fail-closed CI gate via `make build-check` against the PROJECTED .claude/ copy (mirrors how pre-pr.py invokes projected loop-cohort.py). Also: adding a file under packs/core/seeds/ changes the `core` install snapshot — regenerate with UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 pytest tests/integration/test_install_snapshot.py.

title: Prose-path-extraction lints: gate on full rooted paths, exclude placeholder/glob/brace shorthand, pair each exclusion test with an accepting control
body: When a lint extracts file paths from prose (e.g. the spec-metadata lint's invariant (iii) flagging dangling code references in specs), false positives are the dominant risk. Three rules keep it precise: (1) only treat a token as a path if it's a FULL repo-relative path — contains '/', rooted at a known top-level dir (packages/ tools/ packs/ docs/ apps/ .github/) or an explicit ../ / ./ relative link, ending in a recognised extension after stripping a :line/#anchor locator; bare basenames (install.py) can't be located and must be skipped. (2) Exclude non-literal shorthands: placeholders (<...>), globs (*), AND brace-expansion ({a,b}.py) — all denote not-a-single-path. (3) In the self-test, PAIR each exclusion case with a shape-matched ACCEPTING control that DOES fire (e.g. glob `tools/lint-*.py` rejected + `tools/lint-missing.py` warns), so a no-op extractor that matches nothing fails the suite instead of passing every reject vacuously. Keep such a check WARN-ONLY until the corpus warn rate is measured — promoting to hard requires the existing backlog of true positives (often on Frozen specs you can't edit) to be cleaned first. Dogfooding catch: the lint will flag its OWN spec's illustrative example paths — use real resolvable paths (e.g. the lint's own path under packs/core/.apm/skills/work-loop/scripts/) as examples, not tools/x.py.
